A man often has everything he needs for outward enjoyment; yet the Lord leaves him with covetousness or bad qualities so that he does not use what he has well. His possessions end up with strangers somehow; this is vanity, and a harmful condition. A large family was a cherished desire and a mark of honor among the Hebrews; and long life is the desire of people in general. Even with these things a man may not be able to enjoy his wealth, family, and life. Such a man, as he goes through life, seems to have been born for no purpose or benefit. And someone who enters life only for a moment, to leave it the next, has a better situation than someone who has lived long but only to suffer.

A little will serve to sustain us comfortably, and a great deal can do nothing more. The desires of the soul find nothing in the wealth of the world to give satisfaction. A poor man has comfort as well as the richest, and is under no real disadvantage. We cannot say that seeing with your eyes is better than resting your soul in God; for it is better to live by faith in things to come, than to live by sense, which dwells only upon present things. Our lot is appointed. We have what pleases God, and let that please us. The greatest possessions and honors cannot set us above the common events of human life. Seeing that the things people pursue on earth increase vanities, what is a person better for because of their worldly plans? Our life on earth is to be counted by days. It is fleeting and uncertain, and has little in it to cherish or depend on. Let us return to God, trust in his mercy through Jesus Christ, and submit to his will. Then soon we shall pass through this troubling world, and find ourselves in that happy place where there is fullness of joy and pleasures forevermore.
